steps.dialogue: You've learned big ideas today - and you've put them into practice. You worked out that fractions don't just represent parts - they can equal whole numbers too.

You understand that when the numerator equals the denominator, the fraction equals exactly one whole. You can now look at any fraction where the top and bottom numbers match - whether it's [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction], [fraction numerator=10 denominator=10]ten tenths[/fraction], or even [fraction numerator=100 denominator=100]one hundred hundredths[/fraction] - and know that fraction equals 1.
Recognizing patterns and noticing where they appear is a skill that will make you a stronger thinker, in math and beyond.
Did you make a discovery today that surprised you? Maybe that [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] equals 1, or that you could find 1 when given any fraction?

Soon, we'll discover that some fractions can actually equal the SAME amount even when they look different. Your understanding that [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction] equals 1 and [fraction numerator=8 denominator=8]eight eighths[/fraction] equals 1 is going to help you see even more patterns.
See you next time as we continue to explore more about fractions.
